title: The Best Authors Respect the Reader’s Time

full_transcript.speaker: Nivi
full_transcript.text: Unlike Schopenhauer, you're an industrial philosopher, like an industrial designer. Your philosophy is designed for the masses. Like everybody else on Twitter, you're philosophizing for wide adoption. People suggest you read the great books, read Aristotle and Wittgenstein and all the supposedly great philosophers. I mean, I've read almost all that stuff, and I've gotten very little value from it. Where I have gotten value is the philosophizing of people on Twitter like you. Anybody who wants to read philosophy, I would just tell them to skip it and go read David Deutsch.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: You are not wrong. I can't stand any of the philosophers you talked about. I don't like Plato either. Every other piece of philosophy I've picked up and put down relatively quickly because they're just making very obscure arguments over minutiae and trying to come up with all-encompassing theories of the world. Even Schopenhauer falls into that trap when he tries to talk to other philosophers, he's at his worst. When I like him is in his shorter essays. That's where he almost writes like he's on Twitter. He would have dominated Twitter. He has high density of ideas, very well thought through, good minimal examples and analogies. You can pick it up and read one paragraph and you're thinking for the next hour. I think I'm a better writer, a better thinker, and a better judge of people and character thanks to what I read from him. Now, he's writing from the early part of the 19th century. Whenever he wanders into topics that are scientific or medical or political, he's obviously off base. That stuff doesn't apply anymore. But when he's writing about human nature, that is timeless. When it comes to anything about human nature, I say go read the Lindy books, the older books, the ones that have survived the test of time. But if you want to develop specific knowledge, get paid for it, do something useful, then you want to stay on the bleeding edge. That knowledge is going to be more timely and obsolete more quickly. Those two make sense. What doesn't make sense to me is just reading stuff that's not Lindy or that's not about human nature, but is old. I also shy away from stuff that's low density in learning, like history books. I like the Lessons of History by Will Durant because it's a summarization of the Story of Civilization, which was his large 12-volume series. But I'm not going to go read the 12-volume series. I've read plenty of history. I know he's referring to these kinds of things, so I'm not just taking his word for it on high-level concept. But at the same time, at this point in my life, I want to read high-density works. You can call it the TikTok disease or the Twitter generation, but it's also just being respectful of our time. We already have a lot of data, we have some knowledge, now we want wisdom, now we want the generalized principles that we can attach to all of the other information we already have in our minds. We do want to read high-density work, but I would argue that Schopenhauer is very high-density work. All my favorite authors are very high density. Deutsch is extremely high density. Borges is very high density. Ted Chiang is very high density. The old Neal Stephenson was very high density. Then he just got high volume, high density, high everything. But the best authors respect the reader's time, and Schopenhauer certainly did.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: is very much in that vein.

highlights: High-value philosophy should prioritize high-density ideas and generalized principles that respect the reader's time rather than obscure arguments over minutiae.

To understand the timeless aspects of human nature, readers should focus on "Lindy" books that have survived the test of time, while staying on the bleeding edge of modern information for technical or specific knowledge.
Reading should focus on converting existing data and knowledge into wisdom by seeking out authors who provide high-density insights and analogies that can be applied across various fields.
The most effective writers, such as David Deutsch or Jorge Luis Borges, provide a high density of thought per page, allowing a single paragraph to trigger hours of reflection.
summary:
In this transcript, Naval Ravikant and Nivi discuss the practicality of philosophy, questioning the value of traditional "great" philosophers like Plato and Wittgenstein. Both agree that much of classical philosophy is bogged down in obscure minutiae and outdated theories. Naval specifically praises Arthur Schopenhauer’s shorter essays for their "high density" of ideas and timeless insights into human nature, comparing his punchy style to the effectiveness of modern social media. He suggests that while scientific and political views from the past eventually become obsolete, observations on the human condition remain relevant through the "Lindy effect."

Naval outlines a strategic approach to reading, distinguishing between the need for wisdom and the need for utility. For understanding human nature, he recommends "Lindy" books that have survived the test of time; conversely, for developing specific, profitable knowledge, he advises staying on the "bleeding edge" of modern information. Central to his philosophy is a preference for high-density works—such as those by David Deutsch, Jorge Luis Borges, and Ted Chiang—which respect the reader's time by offering generalized principles rather than excessive data. Ultimately, Naval argues that in an age of information overload, the goal is to seek out wisdom and principles that help organize and make sense of the knowledge we already possess.
topics: Timeless wisdom versus timely specific knowledge
Arthur Schopenhauer's influence and density of ideas
